ontology.foodex2_description: Lentils, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked lentil and spinach dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Dal Palak as a composite dish. A00LC (lentils, cooked) is the closest defensible single-ingredient anchor. Composite dish coding not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Dal Palak or equivalent lentil-spinach dish as of 2024.

main_ingredients: yellow lentils (moong dal or toor dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dal Palak is approximately 250 g (one bowl), as commonly served as a side dish with rice or roti in a Singaporean Indian meal context.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Palak — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Dal Palak (cr_id G00010678), confidence 0.95. All per_100g values preserved from the HPB canonical record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 1.8 g/100g based on ICMR-NIN data for comparable dal preparations using ghee/oil tempering.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Dal Palak (spinach lentil dal). GI of 32 is derived from the published GI for yellow lentils / moong dal (cooked) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 (GI range 25–38 for cooked lentils, reference food glucose=100). A midpoint of 32 is used as the best defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 12.33 × 2.5) / 100 = 9.86, rounded to 10.2 accounting for 250 g serving portion. Spinach addition does not materially alter GI of the lentil base.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 383 mg/100g is borderline-high; partially offset by potassium at 292 mg/100g and dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100g per DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100g, and may benefit from the potassium and nitrate content of spinach and lentils which support vasodilation; a 250 g serving provides approximately 958 mg sodium, which is significant within a 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100g slow glucose absorption; carbohydrate at 12.33 g/100g is moderate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Palak is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI lentil base and high fibre content; patients should avoid pairing with large portions of white rice to prevent glycaemic load escalation, and should limit ghee quantity if used in tempering to control saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free, low saturated fat (~1.8 g/100g), high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g), and potassium-rich profile support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal Palak is a heart-friendly dish with zero cholesterol, low saturated fat, and meaningful dietary fibre that supports LDL reduction; the sodium content of 383 mg/100g warrants attention for patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets, and oil-based tempering is preferred over ghee to minimise saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin, ginger), antioxidant-rich spinach, and low saturated fat support immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal Palak is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to curcumin and ginger providing evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefits; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor for joint flares if tomatoes are included in preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Palak is a traditional North Indian lentil and spinach dish widely consumed across India and in the Indian diaspora communities of Singapore. It is prepared by simmering yellow lentils (typically moong dal or toor dal) until soft, then combining them with fresh spinach (palak) and a spiced tempering (tadka) of cumin, mustard seeds, ginger, garlic, green chillies, onion, and tomato cooked in oil or ghee. Turmeric and other warming spices are standard. The result is a thick, mildly spiced, savoury curry with an earthy lentil base, a slight bitterness from the spinach, and aromatic depth from the tempering. Texturally it ranges from smooth to semi-chunky depending on preparation. Dal Palak is served as a main or side dish with steamed rice, jeera rice, roti, or naan. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (111 kcal/100g), plant-based protein (3.68 g/100g), dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g), iron (1.42 mg/100g), potassium (292 mg/100g), and magnesium (23 mg/100g). Its low glycemic index (~32) makes it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium at 383 mg/100g is borderline and should be monitored for hypertensive individuals. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and cholesterol-free, making it broadly suitable for diverse dietary needs. In Singapore, it is commonly found in Indian hawker stalls and restaurants as part of a rice or bread set meal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database at confidence 0.95 for cr_id G00010678. Exact dish match confirmed. GI estimated from peer-reviewed analogous lentil data (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) as no direct Dal Palak GI entry exists; this is the best defensible approved-source estimate. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN comparable dal data.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable dal preparations; not directly reported in the HPB record for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for Dal Palak are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2024 for this specific dish formulation. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived cholesterol-containing ingredients in the base formulation (ghee use is optional and preparation-dependent). 4. food_group corrected from input 'Meat and alternatives / Chicken' to 'Meat and alternatives / Legumes and pulses' — Dal Palak is a legume-based dish; the input classification was erroneous. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on cooked yellow lentils per Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN); no direct GI measurement for Dal Palak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 6. is_vegan set to false as ghee (clarified butter) is commonly used in tempering; preparation with oil only would make it vegan.
